title: Will AI make companies outsource more, or less?
summary_raw: Maybe both.

summary_ai.digest: Noah Smith argues AI will simultaneously drive both outsourcing and in-sourcing, depending on task type and competitive advantage—contradicting the assumption that AI is a simple labor-cost arbitrage play.
summary_ai.summary_long: Smith challenges the binary outsourcing-vs-automation debate by proposing that AI's impact splits along task complexity and strategic value lines. Routine, codifiable work moves to AI or cheaper offshore labor; work requiring coordination, judgment, or proprietary insight stays in-house or reshores. The real constraint becomes talent—who can integrate AI into workflows and own the strategic layer. This reframes how companies think about labor costs (less relevant) versus capability (more relevant), and suggests the next decade favors firms that build internal AI fluency over those that chase lowest-cost offshoring.
talking_points: AI doesn't uniformly kill outsourcing; it kills *commodity* outsourcing—routine, repeatable, low-judgment work that AI or cheaper labor can do equally well.
High-value work—strategy, M&A integration, credit underwriting, relationship management—will concentrate internally or in elite boutiques as companies compete on judgment and speed.
The real constraint post-AI is talent that can operate at the human-AI boundary, not just labor cost, which reshuffles where and how companies organize.
summary_ai.highlight: Outsourcing and in-sourcing both rise—the split is between commodity work (gone) and judgment/coordination work (in-house).

summary_ai.highlight: Cowen identifies a fatal crack in the path-dependence argument: white immigration should have flattened the wealth hierarchy if slavery's legacy alone explained today's gap.
